unverified.label: Empathy as a Cognitive Shortcut
unverified.quote: The only way I can predict your action is to assume first – so a first degree of approximation – that you are like me... that will enable me to avoid the computational hardship of building a new model of yourself.

unverified.label: Free Will as a Computational Illusion
unverified.quote: My position is that there’s no free will, but it’s a very useful illusion... it’s a simple computational problem – would a robot team play better if we equipped them with that language, vocabulary of free will?

unverified.label: Selection Diagrams for Transportability
unverified.quote: A selection diagram adds a node to one of the diagrams saying: 'Here is a factor that creates disparity.' We denote such a node by a square.

unverified.label: The Ladder of Causation
unverified.quote: The ladder of causation consists of three levels. Above them, you have the standard statistics questions... Beyond that, you want to go to level one. What if I do?... Yes, you got almost to the third level of explanation. I know the output. Tell me what caused it.

unverified.label: Deep Learning as a Chinese Room

all_results.label: AI Requires Causal World Models
all_results.quote: My general conclusion is that human-level AI cannot emerge solely from model-blind learning machines; it requires the symbiotic collaboration of data and models.

all_results.label: Insufficiency of Probability Calculus
all_results.quote: The syntax of probability calculus does not permit us to express the simple fact that “symptoms do not cause diseases,” let alone draw mathematical conclusions from such facts.

all_results.label: The Necessity of Untested Causal Assumptions
all_results.quote: one cannot substantiate causal claims from associations alone, even at the population level—behind every causal conclusion there must lie some causal assumption that is not testable in observational studies.

all_results.label: Missing Links Encode Assumptions
all_results.quote: It is important to note that, in path diagrams, causal assumptions are encoded not in the links but, rather, in the missing links.

all_results.label: Statistics and Deep Learning are Limited to Probability

all_results.label: Covariate Selection Requires Causal Knowledge

all_results.quote: all our equations are imprisoned by the connective of equality sign.

all_results.label: The do(x) Operator and Graph Surgery
all_results.quote: This is done through a mathematical operator called do(x) which simulates physical interventions by deleting certain functions from the model, replacing them by a constant X = x, while keeping the rest of the model unchanged.

all_results.label: General Methodology for Causal Inference
all_results.quote: Section 4 outlines a general methodology to guide problems of causal inference: Define, Assume, Identify and Estimate...

all_results.label: Do-calculus
all_results.quote: To elaborate, the do-calculus is a set of rules for manipulating causal expressions, the aim of which is to remove the do-operator from such expressions and reduce them to statistical estimands.

all_results.quote: Given such knowledge, finding a sufficient set of covariates or deciding whether a sufficient set exists are two problems that can readily be solved by graphical methods

all_results.quote: we propose two machine learning models: Exact-MLP and Mask-MLP, which are trained on a small set of reliable subpopulations and are able to predict PNS bounds for all other subpopulations.

all_results.label: The Back-door Criterion
all_results.quote: A set S is admissible (or "sufficient") for adjustment if two conditions hold: 1. No element of S is a descendant of X. 2. The elements of S "block" all "back-door" paths from X to Y...

all_results.label: The First Law of Causal Inference
